original_abstract:
Hybrid porous nanowire arrays, consisting of strongly interacting Co3O4 and carbon, were synthesized via the facile carbonization of a metal-organic framework (MOF) grown directly on Cu foil. Characterization of the resulting material revealed a high surface area of 251 m² g⁻¹ and a significant carbon content of 52.1 wt%. These unique structural features allow the composite to serve as a self-supported working electrode for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), eliminating the need for additional conductive substrates or polymer binders. In alkaline media (0.1 and 1.0 M KOH), the electrode demonstrates exceptional performance, including a low onset potential of 1.47 V (vs. RHE) and a stable current density of 10.0 mA cm⁻² at 1.52 V. Long-term testing in 0.1 M KOH showed the catalyst remains durable for at least 30 hours with a near-unity Faradaic efficiency of 99.3%. 

This superior OER activity and robustness—which outperform many state-of-the-art noble-metal, transition-metal, and nonmetal catalysts—stem from the integrated nanowire array configuration and in situ carbon incorporation. These elements collectively provide a large active surface area, facilitate efficient mass and charge transport, promote the rapid release of oxygen bubbles, and ensure high structural integrity. Additionally, the hybrid Co3O4-carbon arrays function as efficient catalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) via a desirable four-electron pathway. This bifunctionality makes the material a highly promising candidate for reversible oxygen electrodes in rechargeable metal-air batteries, regenerative fuel cells, and other clean energy conversion technologies.
humanized_abstract:
We fabricated hybrid porous nanowire arrays composed of strongly coupled Co3O4 and carbon using a simple method of carbonizing a MOF precursor on Cu foil. The morphological features and detailed compositional information of the product were systemically characterized. The product, with surface area as high as 251 m² g⁻¹ and a high carbon content of 52.1 wt%, acts as a self-supporting working electrode material for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) without requiring any conductive supports or binder polymers. In 0.1 and 1.0 M KOH solutions, the working electrode showed remarkable OER performance including small overpotentials at the onset potentials of 1.47 V vs RHE, and a stable current density of 10.0 mA cm⁻² at potentials of 1.52 V. Such high durability of the working electrode was also confirmed in a long-term durability test in 0.1 M KOH solution with a Faradaic efficiency of almost unity of 99.3% and a catalyst durability time of at least 30 hours.

The extraordinary OER activity and durability of the BCBBC nanocomposite are proven to be the best among many state-of-the-art noble-metal, transition-metal and nonmetal catalysts by utilizing an integrated nanowire array structure with in situ formed carbon. The synergetic function of all components in the nanocomposite leads to the combined effects of a large number of active sites, good mass and charge transport, fast oxygen bubble release, and robust electrode structure, making the hybrid Co3O4-carbon arrays to be efficient catalysts for the ORR to follow the favored 4 electron pathway. Therefore, the BCBBC nanocomposite shows great promise as reversible oxygen electrodes for rechargeable metal-air batteries, regenerative fuel cells, and other clean energy conversion devices.

Such high durability of the working electrode was also confirmed in a long-term durability test in 0.1 M KOH solution with a Faradaic efficiency of almost unity of 99.3% and a catalyst durability time of at least 30 hours.
